Students will be expected to describe and translate among graphic, algebraic, and numeric representations of relations and use those representations to solve problems.
Emphasis will be placed on practical applications and modeling. Appropriate technology, from manipulatives to calculators will be used regularly for instruction and assessment. Honors Algebra II is intended to be more challenging than regular Algebra II and provide multiple opportunities for students to take greater responsibility for their learning, preparing students for AP math courses. This course should be distinguished by a difference in the quality of the work expected rather than merely by the quantity of the work required. In an effort to prepare students for the AP courses, no test retakes or reference sheets will be provided in this course.

Must earn at least a C in the class both semesters to receive 6 credit hours of college credit.
First semester reviews the skills learned in Algebra 2 and then transitions to analysis of graphs of functions, quadratic functions, polynomial functions of higher degree, rational, power, and root functions, and inverse, exponential, and logarithmic functions.
The second semester is the study of trigonometry. Trigonometry is the study of triangles. Topics discussed are solving right triangles and their word problems, graphs of the six trig functions and their inverses, solving oblique triangles, using the trigonometric identities, solving trigonometric equations and the unit circle. No test retakes or reference sheets will be provided in this course. A graphing calculator is required for the course and the TI-84 edition is recommended.

This course will prepare students for the College Board's Advanced Placement exam for Pre-Calculus. Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May. No test retakes or reference sheets will be provided in this course. Graphing calculator is required for the course and the TI-84 edition is recommended.

Probability and Statistics includes the concepts and skills needed to apply statistical techniques in the decision-making process in your everyday life.   Probability and Statistics are made up of three strands: Data Analysis; Experimental Design; and Probability. Practical examples based on real experimental data are used throughout. Students plan and conduct experiments or surveys and analyze the resulting data. The use of graphing technology and computer programs is encouraged.  This class will be a one semester visit into the world of a combination of probability, statistics, and even a touch of quantitative reasoning.  It will be very project based and should give the student a real world sense of where much of the data you read in the newspaper comes from.  This course would be an excellent choice for a senior who needs another semester of math and is college bound but uncertain of their major.

Must earn at least a C in the class to receive 3 credit hours of college credit.
This course studies several theories of mathematics, including matrix theory and determinants, graph theory and color mapping, game theory, election theory, estate division, linear programming and a basic introduction to probability theory. Finite Mathematics takes these theories and techniques and applies them to our society. It looks at how businesses make decisions, social choice, how elections are operated, how mathematics is applied to competition, and how to organize data. Finite Mathematics is structured for those students interested in majoring in the areas of Business, Accounting, Social Sciences, or Liberal Arts, to name a few examples. No test retakes or reference sheets will be provided in this course.

C or better in AP Precalculus or A in DC Precalculus is recommended
This course is an introduction to the major concepts and tools for collecting, analyzing, and drawing conclusions from data. 80% of all college programs require some form of Statistics coursework. This class will prepare students by teaching them the basics of Statistics. Topics that will be included are: methods of data collection, organization of data, and graphical techniques for exhibiting data together with measures of central tendency and variation. Basic laws of probability, sampling theory, hypothesis testing, and making inferences from samples will be included. Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May. No test retakes or reference sheets will be provided in this course. Graphing calculator is required for the course and the TI-84 edition is recommended.

Must earn at least a C in the course to receive 3 credit hours college credit.
This one semester course introduces students to the mathematics required for informed citizenship, decision making, reasoning from evidence, working with real world data, and effective communication. Students will solve problems using proportional reasoning, percentages, rates of change, linear and exponential models with applications from statistics and finance. A good foundation in basic arithmetic as well as solid algebra skills are necessary for success in this class. The big difference between the two QR courses is that this course follows the Ivy Tech syllabus and will be taught with the rigor of a college course. Coming to class with a good work ethic is essential. The course will move faster and delve deeper into the content. No test retakes or reference sheets will be provided in this course.

Must pass a knowledge assessment provided by VU and earn at least a C in the class to receive 5 credit hours of college credit.
Why take dual credit calculus?? As a general rule, dual enrollment is likely to be the more efficient option for earning college credit. There are several advantages...passing a dual enrollment class is guaranteed college and high school credit for you. However, it's not guaranteed that you will be able to transfer the college credits you earn to your targeted 4-year university. Do some research. AP courses could be the edge you need to get into a competitive university or be awarded scholarships. If you plan on applying to Harvard, this is probably not the course for you. The student who takes this course should not be planning a career that is heavy science/math based. This course would be perfect, for example, for the liberal arts majors out there. And if you are a junior, there is nothing stopping you from taking AP Statistics your senior year!! It is not recommended to move from this course into Calculus BC. If you are unsure....take time to have a conversation with your current math teacher. They will be able to give you the facts and let you and your guardian decide what is best for you. No test retakes or reference sheets will be provided in this course. A graphing calculator is required for the course and the TI-84 edition is recommended.

This course will prepare students for the College Board's Advanced Placement exam for Calculus AB. Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May. No test retakes or reference sheets will be provided in this course. A graphing calculator is required for the course and the TI-84 edition is recommended.

This course is focused on the study of numeracy, ratio, and proportional reasoning, modeling, probabilistic reasoning to assess risk, and statistics.  Personal finance and investing money will also be major topics of the course.  Students build knowledge of and confidence with basic mathematical/analytical concepts and operations required for problem-solving, decision-making, and economic productivity in real-world applications and prepare for an increasingly information-based society in which the ability to use and critically evaluate information, especially numerical information, is essential.  Technology, such as computers and graphing calculators, should be used frequently.  QR is structured for students who are planning to pursue a post secondary education but do not need the rigor of the dual credit course based upon their post secondary plans.

This course is a peek into the world of Finite Math. The finite teachers have chosen the topics that are typically most relevant in society and that would prepare a student for success in another more rigorous finite course at the university level. These topics include election theory, estate division, game theory, etc This course would be an excellent choice for a senior who needs another semester of math and is college bound but uncertain of their major. The course is taught predominantly project based with a large amount of group work which is rare in the math world but appealing to many students.

This course will include the topics of calculus along with the study of polynomial approximations and series, series of constants and Taylor series. Students will be prepared for the College Board's Advanced Placement exam for BC calculus.
Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May. No test retakes or reference sheets will be provided in this course. A graphing calculator is required for the course and the TI-84 edition is recommended.

This is a beginner-level, performance-based ensemble for brass and woodwind students. Students can expect to learn the fundamentals of musicianship and develop good ensemble playing techniques. After-school commitments include band concerts, contests, and rehearsals leading up to these performances. Attendance at all rehearsals and performances is mandatory. Students should consider replacing their beginner instrument with a step-up instrument for this class. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the band boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class

This is an intermediate-level, performance-based ensemble for brass, woodwind, and percussion students. Students can expect to refine the fundamentals of musicianship and develop advanced ensemble playing techniques. After-school commitments include band concerts, contests, and rehearsals leading up to these performances. Attendance at all rehearsals and performances is mandatory. Students should replace their beginner instrument with a step-up instrument. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the band boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class

This is an intermediate-level, performance-based ensemble for brass, woodwind, and percussion students. Students can expect to refine the fundamentals of musicianship and develop advanced ensemble playing techniques. After-school commitments include band concerts, contests, and rehearsals leading up to these performances. Attendance at all rehearsals and performances is mandatory. Students should replace their beginner instrument with a step-up instrument. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the band boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

This is an advanced-level, performance-based ensemble for brass, woodwind, and percussion students. This ensemble serves as the premier performing ensemble of the band program. Students can expect to perfect the fundamentals of musicianship and develop highly advanced ensemble playing techniques. After-school commitments include band concerts, contests, and rehearsals leading up to these performances. Attendance at all rehearsals and performances is mandatory. Every member should be playing a high quality instrument. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the band boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

This is a beginner-level, performance-based class for percussion students. Students can expect to learn the fundamentals of musicianship and develop good ensemble playing techniques. After-school commitments include band concerts, contests, and rehearsals leading up to these performances. Attendance at all rehearsals and performances is mandatory. All students must have the appropriate mallets and sticks, purchased at the expense of the student, as determined by the instructor. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the band boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

This is an advanced-level, performance-based class for percussion students. Students can expect to learn the fundamentals of musicianship and develop good ensemble playing techniques. After-school commitments include band concerts, contests, and rehearsals leading up to these performances. Attendance at all rehearsals and performances is mandatory. All students must have the appropriate mallets and sticks, purchased at the expense of the student, as determined by the instructor. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the band boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

This is an advanced-level, performance-based class for percussion students. Students can expect to learn the fundamentals of musicianship and develop good ensemble playing techniques. After-school commitments include band concerts, contests, and rehearsals leading up to these performances. Attendance at all rehearsals and performances is mandatory. All students must have the appropriate mallets and sticks, purchased at the expense of the student, as determined by the instructor. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the band boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

This is an advanced-level, performance-based ensemble for students in the high school band program. Students can expect to learn the fundamentals of jazz music and develop advanced ensemble playing techniques. Students will play a variety of jazz styles including rock, Latin, and swing music. After-school commitments include band concerts, contests, and other miscellaneous performance opportunities. Attendance at all practices and performances is mandatory. Students must be enrolled in another band class to enroll in this course. Exceptions to this, such as students who play guitar, bass guitar, drum set, or piano, may be granted at the discretion of the instructor. Instrumentation is limited to traditional big band instrumentation: saxophone, trumpet, trombone, guitar, bass guitar, percussion, and piano. Exceptions to this instrumentation policy are at the sole discretion of the director. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the band boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

Dance Choreography focuses on the development of the specific skills related to the color guard activity. Course content will include music terminology, melodic content and contrast, music listening, performance techniques, choreography, costuming and makeup, understanding the correlation between meter and tempo of music as it relates to choreography, and safety practices (warm up and cool down). Students enrolled in this course are required to be a part of the marching band. Tryouts and registration for the marching band / Color Guard occurs in the spring of the previous school year.

Descants is an entry-level vocal course for treble voices designed to improve vocal technique and music literacy.  Students will sing music in two or more parts from various styles and cultures.  Academic course content, attendance at school performances and ISSMA contests, and rehearsals are mandatory.

Vocal Majority is an entry-level vocal course for tenor/bass voices designed to improve vocal technique and music literacy.   Students will sing music in two or more parts from various styles and cultures. Academic course content, attendance at school performances and ISSMA contests, and rehearsals are mandatory.

Flight is an entry-level concert and competitive show choir course for treble voices designed to improve vocal technique and music literacy.  Placement in this ensemble is based upon a vocal and choreography audition.  This course is co-curricular and functions as both a concert and extracurricular competitive show choir.  This course will require students to pay for an extracurricular show choir.  There are several fundraising and financial aid opportunities to assist in payment.  Students will sing music in two or more parts from various styles and cultures.  Academic course content, attendance at school performances and ISSMA contests, and rehearsals are mandatory.

Encores is an intermediate-level concert and competitive show choir course for treble and tenor/bass voices designed to improve vocal technique and music literacy.  Placement in this ensemble is based upon a vocal and choreography audition.  This course is co-curricular and functions as a concert and extracurricular competitive show choir. Students will have after school rehearsals and will attend performances beyond the regular concert schedule.  This course will require students to pay for an extracurricular show choir.  There are several fundraising and financial aid opportunities to assist in payment.  Students will sing in four or more parts from various styles and cultures. Academic course content, attendance at school performances and ISSMA contests, and rehearsals are mandatory.

Harmonia is an intermediate-level vocal course for treble voices designed to improve vocal technique and music literacy.  Students will sing music in three or more parts from various styles and cultures. Academic course content, attendance at school performances and ISSMA contests, and rehearsals are mandatory.

Ambassadors is an advanced-level vocal course for tenor/bass voices designed to improve vocal technique and music literacy.  Placement in this ensemble is based upon a vocal audition.  This course will require students to pay for an extracurricular choir.  There are several fundraising and financial aid opportunities to assist in payment.  Students will sing music in three or more parts from various styles and cultures.  Academic course content, attendance at school performances and ISSMA contests, and rehearsals are mandatory.

High Voltage is an advanced-level ensemble of treble voices who work on a wide range of vocal styles from classical concert literature to popular and show choir music.  Placement in this ensemble is based upon a vocal and choreography audition.  This course is co-curricular and functions as both a concert and extracurricular competitive show choir. This group typically rehearses Tuesdays and Thursdays after school for three hours, and attends day-long competitions on most Saturdays January through March.  This course will require students to pay for an extracurricular show choir.  There are several fundraising and financial aid opportunities to assist in payment.  Students will sing music in three or more parts from various styles and cultures.  Academic course content, attendance at school performances and ISSMA contests, and rehearsals are mandatory.

FC Singers is an advanced-level ensemble of treble and tenor/bass voices who perform a wide range of vocal styles from classical concert literature to popular and show choir music.  Placement in this ensemble is based upon a vocal and choreography audition.  This course is co-curricular and functions as both a concert and extracurricular competitive show choir.  This group typically rehearses Tuesdays and Thursdays after school for three hours, and attends day-long competitions on most Saturdays from January through March.  This course will require students to pay for an extracurricular show choir.  There are several fundraising and financial aid opportunities to assist in payment.  Students will sing music in four or more parts from various styles and cultures. Academic course content, attendance at school performances and ISSMA contests, and rehearsals are mandatory.

Accents is an advanced-level ensemble of treble singers who work on a wide range of vocal styles from Renaissance madrigals and motets to modern pop and vocal jazz.  Placement in this ensemble is based upon a vocal audition.  This course will require students to pay for extracurricular choir such as travel to out of state competitions.  There are several fundraising and financial aid opportunities to assist in payment.  Students will sing music in four or more parts from various styles and cultures. Academic course content and attendance at school performances/rehearsals are mandatory.

Chamber Choir is a highly select group of treble and tenor/bass singers who work on a wide range of vocal styles from Renaissance madrigals and motets to modern pop and vocal jazz.  Placement in this ensemble is based upon a vocal audition.  This course will require students to pay for extracurricular choir such as travel to out of state competitions.  There are several fundraising and financial aid opportunities to assist in payment.  Students will sing music in four or more parts from various styles and cultures.  Academic course content and attendance at school performances and rehearsals are mandatory.

This orchestra will be rooted in the fundamentals and technique of performing in a music ensemble. Students will also begin to develop skills and artistry in musical expression. Membership is primarily composed of freshmen recommended by the orchestra director. In addition to developing technique, this course will focus on group III and IV repertoire as defined by the <u>I</u>ndiana <u>S</u>tate <u>S</u>chool <u>M</u>usic <u>A</u>ssociation (ISSMA). Fundamentals of music theory will complement the performance material. All Cadet Orchestra students will be strongly urged but not required to participate in the ISSMA Solo and Ensemble contest. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the orchestra boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

This orchestra will be rooted in the fundamentals and technique of performing in a music ensemble. Students will also begin to develop skills and artistry in musical expression. Membership is primarily composed of musicians 10th grade and above recommended by the orchestra director. In addition to developing technique, this course will focus on group III repertoire as defined by the Indiana State School Music Association (ISSMA). Fundamentals of music theory will complement the performance material. All Academy Orchestra students will be strongly urged but not required to participate in the ISSMA Solo and Ensemble contest. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the orchestra boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

This intermediate-level orchestra will be rooted in the fundamentals and technique of performing in a music ensemble.  Students will also begin to develop skills and artistry in musical expression.  Membership is primarily composed of freshmen and some sophomores recommended by the orchestra director.  In addition to developing technique, this course will focus on group II and III repertoire as defined by the Indiana State School Music Association (ISSMA).  Fundamentals of music theory will complement the performance material.  All Symphony Orchestra students will be strongly urged but not required to participate in the ISSMA Solo and Ensemble contest. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the orchestra boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

This advanced-level orchestra is composed of the most experienced members of the orchestra program.  Membership is comprised of students recommended by the orchestra director. Students in Camerata Orchestra will study and perform top-level repertoire for string orchestra from all musical time periods. In the winter semester, students will rehearse and perform with select wind and percussion players as a Philharmonic Orchestra for ISSMA State Qualifiers. Placement in this ensemble includes 1-2 after-school rehearsals per week during winter semester. Camerata may also perform as a separate string orchestra for Large Group Contest, pending adequate progress. This performance-driven course is rooted in the development of musical expression. Curriculum enrichments, such as TED talks, recordings and videos of musical performances, texts, and literature highlighting performance practices are used to engage this course. Camerata Orchestra students are strongly urged to study privately with professional musicians. Participation in ISSMA Solo and Ensemble is highly recommended. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the orchestra boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

This group will explore performing styles outside traditional Classical music, including but not limited to: Electrified Strings (Blitz!), Jazz/Pops Orchestra, American/Irish/Scottish Fiddle, Mariachi, and Theater Pit Orchestra. Students will also explore improvised solos over chord changes. Jazz string orchestra arrangements will be performed at ISSMA Jazz Contest during Winter Quarter.  Pending availability, students in this course will occasionally collaborate with members of the Jazz Bands on pops orchestra and/or theater pit orchestra performances. Placement in this ensemble includes after-school rehearsals as needed for performances, including extensive rehearsals approaching staged theater productions, as well as multiple evening/weekend performances. This course has a small, per semester fee paid to the orchestra boosters to offset expenses incurred during the class.

Theatre Arts is a one-semester survey class in which students will primarily develop acting skills.  Students will develop acting skills through pantomime, monologues, partner scenes, reading scripts, and learning how to analyze plays and identity characteristics.  In addition to acting, students will explore scenic design and short script creation.

Technical Theatre will actively engage students in the process of designing, building, managing, and implementing the aspects of a production. Students will explore stage technology and make informed judgments about technical production choices.  Students will develop scenic designs, lighting, costuming, make-up, sound, and stage and house management. Students will understand the ways technical theater incorporates all the arts and other disciplines through construction, business, project management, design, and advertising. Students explore career opportunities in theatre and attend and critique theatrical productions.

Advanced Technical Theatre will actively lead students in the process of design, building, managing, programming, drafting, and implementing the technical aspects of a production. Students will create original designs and plans for scenic design, lighting, costuming, make-up, sound, and stage and house management. Further, students will assess stage technology and its impact on the theatre and their designs and implementation of technical elements of the production. Students will investigate careers in technical theatre and attend and critique theatrical productions.

Do you enjoy acting or doing behind the scenes technical work? What about putting those skills into competition? If so, Production is for you!
Theatre Production is a year-long class in which students will take on responsibilities associated with rehearsing and presenting a fully mounted theatre production. They will read and analyze plays in preparation for productions. Students will conceive a design for a production including set, lighting, sound, costumes, make-up, house management, and advertising. Students will investigate a theatre arts career and attend and critique theatrical productions. Students will rehearse and perform roles in a production, direct or serve as assistant director for a production and focus on the responsibilities associated with presenting a full scale theatre production. Students will employ a careful process of script analysis to create a character, and fully engage in the collaborative process of making theatre. Lastly, students will put all of these skills to the test by competing in regional and state thespian competitions. Whether you compete on stage or off- after school time is required as well as fees and travel expenses for competitions. Theatre Production is a great place to get a feel for what real, professional theatre is like.

Students enrolled in Advanced Acting will research, create, and perform characters through script analysis, observation, collaboration and rehearsal. These activities will incorporate elements of theatre history, culture, analysis, response, creative process and integrated studies.

This class trains the integration of dance and voice techniques as required by the Musical Theatre and Show Choir performance genres. Students will be taught to sing and dance at the same time while still performing efficiently, expressively, and effectively.. This class is taught by a theatre teacher with an additional dance instructor. Repertoire will be chosen from the Broadway canon to train students to become singing-actors. The semester will conclude with a public cabaret performance to showcase student growth.
NOTE: there is a required performance element to this class which must be completed in order to pass

An introductory course in directing for the stage. Topics include the role and function of the director in the contemporary theater; the basic tools of proscenium blocking and staging, such as composition, picturization, movement, and gesture; structural script analysis; and basic actor coaching techniques.

An exploration of the physiology and acoustics of the human singing voice in a group setting. Vocal techniques are taught to establish a healthy foundation for solo performance that enhances efficiency and longevity of the vocal mechanism. Repertoire will be chosen from the Broadway canon to train students to become singing-actors. The semester will conclude with a public cabaret performance to showcase student growth.
NOTE: There is a required performance element to this class which must be completed in order to pass.

The aim of this course is for each student to write various short scripts to the best of their ability as well as one final One Act play. It is open to students both with a theater background and those without. This course will involve a number of preliminary exercises, the preparation of a scenario, the development of the material through individual conferences, and finally the reading and discussion of the student's work in seminar sessions.

The purpose of Theatre Appreciation is to develop understanding, appreciation and critical perceptions of the theatrical event.  The course will approach theatre as an art form, an entertainment medium and as a vehicle for self-expression.  Emphasis will be placed on the history of theatre, acting, directing, playwriting, theatre technology, costume design, scenic design, and lighting design.  Active participation in the playwriting, acting, directing and designing processes will be provided.  The course will also require attendance at theatrical events to offer firsthand experience of theatre arts.

Introduction to Music Theory and Composition is an elective course for members of performing arts ensembles and other students who want to understand music construction and function.  Music theory distills and analyzes the fundamental elements of music:  melody, harmony, harmonic function, rhythm, form, and texture.  In addition to the written aspects of music, aural skills or the ability to identify musical patterns by ear, are integral to the course.  Students will have to dictate played music and sing music at sight using solfege syllables.  Students who wish to continue on to AP Music Theory and are not currently enrolled in an advanced performing ensemble at Franklin Central should consider this course.

Music Theory is the study of the structure of constructed music. Music theorists look for patterns and structures in composers' works across or within genres, styles, or historical periods. Music theory distills and analyzes the fundamental parameters or elements of music:  melody, harmony, harmonic function, rhythm, form, and texture.  In addition to the written aspects of music, aural skills or the ability to identify musical patterns by ear, as opposed to by the reading of notation are integral to the course. Students will dictate played music and read music at sight. It is recommended, although not required, that students have completed at least one year of a Franklin Central advanced ensemble (Symphony Orchestra, Wind Ensemble, FC Singers, High Voltage, Accents or Chamber Choir) before enrolling in AP Music Theory. Students will take the AP Music Theory exam in May.

Music History and Appreciation is a study of music from early civilizations to the twenty-first century. The course will focus on musical periods of history and the composers and music therein. Students should come prepared for a history course requiring reading and studying. Previous musical experience is helpful, but not required. Students can expect the following academic standards to be addressed: singing alone and with others, reading and notating music, evaluating and describing music, understanding the relationship between music and the other arts, and understanding music in relation to history and culture. Written assignments and presentations in front of the class are required. Through the study of music history, students will gain an appreciation of varied styles and genres of music and develop an educated musical ear.

Biology is an introductory course designed to study living organisms and their physical environment. Students should apply scientific methods of inquiry and research in examining the following areas: biochemistry, cell structure, function and reproduction, cell energy, molecular basis of genetics, natural selection and diversity, and ecology. This course is required for graduation.

Honors Biology is designed for students who have excellent reading, comprehension, and study skills. The pace in the course is rapid, and critical thinking is stressed. Biology is a laboratory/project-based course designed to study living organisms and their physical environment.  Students should apply scientific methods of inquiry and research in examining the following topics: biochemistry, cell structure and function, cell energy, molecular basis of genetics, natural selection and diversity, and ecology. This course is required for graduation.

Integrated Chemistry and Physics (ICP) is an engaging course that explores key principles of both Chemistry and Physics through hands-on and conceptual learning. The class moves at a steady pace and encourages students to think critically, problem-solve, and make real-world connections. With consistent effort and curiosity, all students can find success and even excel in this course.

Chemistry I is designed primarily for the college bound student.  It is a mathematics and laboratory based science class that covers topics such as atomic structure, chemical reactions, stoichiometry, solutions, gas laws, and acids and bases.  Proficiency in Algebra I skills is required and more advanced mathematics is strongly recommended.

Early College Chemistry is a laboratory-based science course designed for Early College Cohort students.  Students will explore the principles that explain how matter behaves and changes. The course covers key topics such as atomic structure, chemical reactions, stoichiometry, solutions, gas laws, and acids and bases. Students will develop their problem-solving and critical-thinking skills through hands-on experiments and inquiry-based learning.  Proficiency in Algebra I skills is required and more advanced mathematics is strongly recommended.

Honors Chemistry I is designed primarily for the self-motivated student with strong reading comprehension, critical thinking, and study skills.  It is a rapid-paced mathematics and laboratory based science class that utilizes inquiry to cover topics such as atomic structure, chemical reactions, stoichiometry, solutions, gas laws, and acids and bases.  Proficiency in Algebra I skills is required and more advanced mathematics is strongly recommended.

Physics is the search for the fundamental laws that govern the behavior of objects in the universe. It's about the nature of basic things such as motion, forces, energy, matter, electricity, magnetism, and the composition of atoms. The emphasis will be on developing a conceptual understanding of these laws and phenomena. The course will give the students a deeper appreciation for the beauty of the natural world as revealed by modern science.

This course is the equivalent of a first-semester college course in algebra-based physics.  The course covers Newtonian mechanics, work, energy, power, and mechanical waves and sound.  It also includes heat and thermodynamic principles and fluid dynamics for the Ivy Tech Physics 101 credit.  Through inquiry-based learning, students will develop scientific critical thinking and reasoning skills.  Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May.

This course is designed to help students explore the world and universe around them.  Students will master a wide variety of Earth Science topics including meteorology (atmosphere, weather, storms), geology (minerals, rocks, plate tectonics, volcanoes, and earthquakes), astronomy (sun/Earth/moon, planets, stars and galaxies). Students analyze and describe Earth's interconnected systems and examine how Earth's materials, landforms, and continents are modified throughout time. Labs and hands-on activities will help students relate science to everyday life.

Students will investigate and apply concepts associated with human anatomy and physiology. Concepts covered include the process of homeostasis and the essentials of human function at the level of genes, cells, tissues, and organ systems. Students will understand the structure, organization, and function of the various components of the healthy human body in order to apply this knowledge in all health-related fields. Animal dissection is included as part of the laboratory component of the course.

This course will explore the structure, function, diversity and evolution of the ten major phyla of the animal kingdom. Students will be expected to participate in the dissection of organisms from each phyla. The primary focus will be a systematic study of the animal phyla to provide students with an understanding of the interaction between structure and function and an understanding of the evolutionary advancements exhibited by each phylum. The course will be a mixture of lectures, dissections, and labs. Class activities and laboratories will include different animal dissections, animal and human diseases, ecology and biodiversity studies, and physiological studies of animals. This course would be great for sophomores, juniors, or seniors who enjoyed biology, are considering vet or medical school, or are looking for a fun, interactive science class!

Forensic Science is an application of science (chemistry, physics, and biology) to the criminal and civil laws that are enforced by police agencies in a criminal justice system.  This course will focus on the application of science in the analysis of evidence collected at crime scenes as well as during autopsies.  Topics covered in this course are: history of forensic science, crime scene drawing and investigation, evidence collection and analyzing, fingerprinting, blood and blood spatter analysis, forensic toxicology, and hair and fiber analysis in both a laboratory setting and in case studies.  Sensitivity warning:  Students will be exposed to and research true crime (both historic and current) which includes many sensitive and trigger warning topics

In the first semester of Science Research, students will practice and learn various lab techniques while designing their own research project and writing their own research paper. Types of lab techniques that will be used in the course include gel electrophoresis, the use of microscopes, aseptic technique, the use of statistical analysis, chromatography, and more. The research projects are picked and designed from the ground up by the students. By the end of the first semester, students should have written a background research paper and have a fully designed experiment.
In the second semester, students will follow the plan they designed for their research project and conduct their experiments. They will record their findings and create a presentation for the regional science fair in which they will compete. Depending on student performance, some students will be able to compete in higher level science fairs. After the science fairs, students will review their feedback from the judges and modify their experimental design.

class_name: Bioethics and the Biology of Happiness
description:
The first semester covers the biology of happiness. This portion of the course studies the physiological underpinnings of the psychological experience of happiness. The course is split into theory and practice. In regard to theory, students learn how the body is built and operates, from the ground up. Students learn how what people eat becomes incorporated into human bodies, how the body is built and operates, and how the nervous and endocrine systems work together to create thought and emotion. Students learn how humans get to the point of thinking and feeling.
The other main component to the study of happiness is practice. After learning about the physiology of happiness, students put what they know into action. Students use evidence-based practices that promote happiness. Students take action to promote their own happiness. The course culminates in a student-designed and conducted project that promotes happiness.
The second semester covers bioethics. In this portion of the course, students take part in inquiry through in-depth extended literature reviews and analysis of biological concepts and their implications in numerous ethical scenarios. Students learn relevant biological principles as well as traditional philosophical schools of thought in order to discern bioethical principles. This portion of the course integrates concepts from anatomy/physiology, botany, ecology, biochemistry, and other disciplines in order to garner a greater understanding of the intersection of biology and ethics. This portion of the course also culminates in a student-designed action project focused on a selected bioethical issue.

This course introduces the study of plants and animals and how they interact within ecosystems. Students will learn how scientists identify and classify living organisms, examine key structures and functions, and explore how plants and animals depend on one another and their environments to survive.
Hands-on lab activities and dissection are a major part of this course.
Emphasis is placed on the importance of these relationships to the environment and to human life. By the end of the course, students will have a solid understanding of biological diversity and the connections that sustain life on Earth.

AP Environmental Science is a rigorous and fast-paced course covering aspects of national and global environmental systems, including ecosystems, population dynamics, resource management, and environmental consequences of natural and anthropogenic processes. The course is a good fit for all students interested in science and highly recommended for students considering a career related to Earth or Environmental Science. Laboratory and field experiences are required components of the course. Computer, Internet, math, and reading skills are necessary. Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May.

This is a rigorous and fast paced course intended for students pursuing advanced credit in the life sciences. It is intended to prepare students for the College Board's Advanced Placement exam, as well as eight mandatory labs that will be completed during the course.  Summer work may be assigned prior to the beginning of school in August. Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May and must meet Vincennes University prerequisites in order to earn Dual Credit.

This is an advanced placement course designed to prepare the student for the AP Chemistry exam. This course covers the equivalent of one full year of college level general chemistry, comparable to a first year course at a college or university. This course is a rigorous math-based course, with a strong laboratory component. It is intended for students who have demonstrated a willingness to commit considerable time to studying and completing assignments outside of class, and who have successfully completed Honors Chemistry I or Chemistry I, and Algebra II. This course includes more in-depth analysis of material covered in Chemistry I, as well as new topics:  kinetics, equilibrium, thermodynamics, electrochemistry, and nuclear chemistry.  Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May and must meet Ivy Tech prerequisites in order to earn Dual Credit.  The Knowledge Assessment from Ivy Tech for precalculus is a prerequisite for dual credit at Ivy Tech.

This course is the equivalent of a second-semester college course in algebra-based physics. The course covers fluid mechanics, Thermodynamics, electricity and magnetism, optics, and atomic and nuclear physics. Through inquiry-based learning, students will develop scientific critical thinking and reasoning skills. Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May.

course_name: Geography and History of the World
description: This two-semester course will serve as the standard social studies requirement for freshmen. Students will use geographical skills and historical concepts to deepen their understanding of global themes. Students will use research tools to ask questions and acquire relevant information. They will use primary and secondary sources, produce maps, timelines, and other graphic representations to interpret geographic and historical problems and events. Key concepts include change over time, cultural landscape, diffusion, human-environment interactions, physical systems, and spatial organization and variation.

course_name: Geography and History of the World Early College
description: This course is a part of our Early College curriculum and is designed as a college preparatory course.  The time commitment, rigor, and levels of critical thinking will increase as the year progresses.  This will help to prepare the students for dual credit courses in the future.  The course will be taught in tandem with the Early College English 9 curriculum: some assignments will be cross-curricular.  Students will use geographical skills and historical concepts to deepen their understanding of global themes. Students will use research tools to ask questions and acquire relevant information. They will use primary and secondary sources, produce maps, timelines, and other graphic representations to interpret geographic and historical problems and events. Key concepts include change over time, cultural landscape, diffusion, human-environment interactions, physical systems, and spatial organization and variation.

course_name: Geography and History of the World Honors
description: This two-semester course will serve as an introduction to Advanced Placement social studies courses for freshmen.  This course demands the academic excellence expected from an Advanced Placement course, including the development of advanced study skills and rigorous amounts of out of school work.  Students should be prepared to make a serious commitment of time and energy to make the most of the course.  In the class, students will use geographical skills and historical concepts to deepen their understanding of global themes.  Students will develop their non-fiction literacy skills by analyzing primary and secondary sources.  Students will also collaboratively use research tools to ask questions and acquire relevant information.  They will produce maps, timelines, and other graphic representations to interpret geographic and historical problems and events.  Key concepts include change over time, cultural landscape, diffusion, human-environment interactions, physical systems, and spatial organization and variation.

This course is designed to be the equivalent of a two-semester introductory college or university world history course. According to the College Board, AP World History Modern students 'investigate significant events, individuals, developments, and processes in historical periods from approximately 1200 CE to the present.' Students develop and use the same skills, practices, and methods employed by historians: analyzing primary and secondary sources; making historical comparisons; utilizing reasoning about contextualization, causation, and continuity and change over time; and developing historical arguments. In addition to utilizing the listed skills, students are required to write three long essays per quarter in preparation for their AP course exam. Students should anticipate 5-7 hours of homework per week consisting mostly of reading and notes.  Students are expected to come prepared to class sessions with all work completed. Students not willing to meet the work requirements are strongly discouraged from signing up for this course.  Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May to receive college credit.

World History is a year-long course that begins with the Renaissance and ends with the fall of communism. Students will explore major historical events/perspectives, political systems of the world, changing geographic boundaries, the impact of colonialism, industrialization, and political revolutions/independence, cause/effects of wars, global dominance and globalization.  This course will provide students with an overview of the events and individuals who have shaped our world as we know it today.  Students will use a variety of methods that include primary/secondary sources, group/individual project-based learning activities, small group cooperative learning activities, research-based activities, guided learning that leads to independent learning, and Canvas activities. Students will be exposed to multiple perspectives, ideas and approaches. This course requires students to be actively engaged in discussions, projects and overall coursework.

United States History is a required two-semester course designed for all juniors. US History will expand upon concepts learned in prior American History courses and emphasize national development from the Civil War period through the twenty-first century. Students will learn through a variety of sources including primary sources and secondary sources, historical and contemporary sources, internet activities, music, film, and research. Students will study common themes throughout America's history, key events and people, various movements, and how all related to life in the United States and in Indiana.  The class will require students to read and develop note-taking skills.  The course is primarily structured to evaluate individual performance.  Occasional partner or group projects will be utilized in order to promote teamwork and communication skills.

class_name: Dual Credit AP United States History
description: AP Dual Credit U.S.  History (APUSH) is designed for students looking to challenge themselves beyond the required U.S. History course. APUSH is a full-year rigorous, college level course designed to explore United States history from 1491 (pre-Columbian) to the present. Students who elect this course should possess strong reading, writing, and organizational skills. The course will emphasize and focus on the development of analytical and writing skills necessary for success at the collegiate level. This will be done, at least partly, through the critical evaluation of primary and secondary sources and analysis of historiographical arguments. At the end of the course students will take the AP US History exam administered by the College Board. Success on the exam (i.e. scoring a 3, 4 or 5) can lead to college credit, depending on the specific policies of the college they choose to attend. This course is also accredited through Vincennes University and passage grants the student transferable credit from Vincennes for BOTH Hist 139 and Hist 140 amounting to 6 college credits. Course assessments in this class are closely aligned with the assessments on the AP Exam. Students in this course should expect homework and/or reading every day.

This course, required of all seniors, will include an investigation of the mechanics and operation of the various political institutions of our nation. The role of citizens in their government, as well as the Constitution, will be the major focus of the course while other areas such as history, geography, economics, and cultures will play a supporting role. This course is the culmination of the thinking, inquiry and research, and participation skill developed throughout the social studies curriculum. Political participation, the role of the courts, the Presidency, the Congress, and civil liberties are major units of study. A number of learn-and-do or learn-by-doing activities such as Mock Congress are part of the class.  Also included are small group cooperative learning activities, internet learning, and Canvas activities. Students will be exposed to all levels of society in an effort to properly prepare them for the social realities of the modern political world.

class_name: Dual Credit Government We the People
description: US Government Honors follows the 'We the People': The Citizen and the Constitution curriculum, and it is a one-semester honors course, offered during the fall, that counts towards the required Government credit; it is intended for college-bound students who have a passion for politics, history, debate, discussion, speech, philosophy, hands-on-learning, and civics. The course introduces students to a study of constitutional law in the United States of America. Its intent and purpose is to provide students with an understanding of how the Constitution came into existence, why it took the form it did, and how it has functioned over the past two hundred years. The Center for Civic Education hosts regional, state, and national competitions based on the course curriculum, and it is required for students to take part, participate, and compete in the competitions, and this aspect of the course will require students to dedicate time outside of class to the course. Because space is limited, teacher selection through an application process is required. REQUIRED APPLICATION

This course, required of all seniors, is designed to stimulate the students' awareness of, and continuing interest in, economics. Economics is the study of decision making, and an understanding of economic principles will enable students to think intelligently and analytically relative to a variety of modern-day economic problems. Individual units apply this information to a variety of policy problems such as poverty, taxes, pollution, economic growth, money/banking, gross domestic product, as well as prices and interest rate changes. Students will gain a fundamental understanding of economic markets, and demonstrate this by means of a special project. Other course projects help students develop skills in the areas of Personal Finance, Media Literacy, Global Thinking and Project Planning.

This one-semester course is recommended to highly motivated students who plan to major in economics or business in college. All materials used in this course are from universities throughout the country, as are the exams. Completion of Algebra II is recommended.  An understanding of economics will enable students to master a few basic economic problems as a means of thinking intelligently and analytically relative to a variety of policy problems such as aggregate demand/supply, economic growth, deficits, debts, interest rates, loans, and international trade. Students should expect 2-3 hours of homework per week for this course, the majority of which will involve college-level reading and practice problems. Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May.

To truly understand the world today, it must be viewed within a spatial context, or the 'where and why things occur.' Human geography is the study of where humans and their activities and institutions such as ethnic groups, cities, and industries are located and why they are there. Human geographers also study the interactions of humans with their environment and draw on some basic elements of physical geography. The purpose of the AP course in Human Geography is to introduce students to the systematic study of patterns and processes that have shaped human understanding, use, and alteration of Earth's surface. Students employ spatial concepts and landscape analysis to examine human social organization and its environmental consequences. They also learn about the methods and tools geographers use in their science and practice. This is a full year, college level geography course. This course is recommended to seniors and juniors with an interest in geography and society, and is open to highly motivated sophomores as well. Students should expect 4-6 hours of homework each week, including college level readings and writing. This course is highly project-oriented and will require students to collaborate with each other on a regular basis. Students signing up for this course are required to take the AP exam in May.

AP European History explores the impact of Europe from the 1200s to modern day through readings, essays, primary sources, and projects. This course is a college level so students should be prepared for heavy reading and writing in the class. This course is recommended for juniors and seniors, as an elective, who are prepared for nightly homework from a textbook in addition to other assignments. Registration for this course requires students to take the A.P. European History exam in May for an attempt at college credit.

This one-semester course is an introduction to the theories and concepts of sociology, the study of groups. This class illustrates how the groups to which you belong (by choice or 'assignment') have a profound influence on the way you think, feel, and act. Sociology looks at groups rather than individuals. Major themes include culture, socialization, race and ethnicity, deviance and social control, crime and punishment, the inequities of age, gender, and social class, the teen years and the social agents of religion, education and the family.  Coursework includes readings from a variety of sources and viewpoints, small and large group discussions.  Assessments are project based.

Indiana has played a vital role in the development of our nation's history, economy, government and culture.  This course highlights the Hoosier people, places, culture and events, past and present.   It is designed for students looking for an opportunity to study Indiana history with a deeper, more focused approach through reading, writing, discussion, and mini-projects. Topics covered include: Indiana geography, native peoples, immigration, economics, politics, culture (art, literature and music), and the role of conflict in shaping Indiana's history, as well as the discussion of current topics in Indiana. Learn more about the accomplishments of our state and its people.

Ethnic Studies provides opportunities to broaden students' perspectives concerning cultural, geographical, political and socioeconomic patterns of ethnic groups in the United States. This course will focus on the experiences of Indigenous Americans, African Americans, Latinx Americans, and Asian Americans and Pacific Islanders (AAPI). We will examine the historical and contemporary experiences of aforementioned groups using primary/secondary sources, project based learning, presentations, small group cooperative learning, research, and Canvas activities. Students will be exposed to multiple perspectives, ideas and approaches. This course requires students to be actively engaged in discussions, projects, presentations, and overall coursework.
Ethnic Studies Course Spotlight

AP Psychology is based on content established by the AP College Board and is designed to be equivalent to an introductory college course. This year-long course will introduce students to the systematic and scientific study of behavior and mental processes and will prepare students for success on the AP Psychology Exam. Topics include: (1) History and Approaches, (2) Research Methods, (3) Biological Bases of Behavior, (4) Sensation and Perception, (5) States of Consciousness, (6) Learning, (7) Cognition, (8) Motivation and Emotion, (9) Developmental Psychology, (10) Personality, (11) Testing and Individual Differences, (12) Clinical Psychology, and (13) Social Psychology. Students signing up for this class should possess strong reading, writing, critical thinking, and discussion abilities. Students should expect several hours of reading and homework per week for this course. It is expected that students are prepared to accept the responsibility of a college level course. Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP Exam in May to earn college credit.

This course is intended for college-bound students who are interested in examining the various perspectives in psychology. Psychology is the scientific study of behavior and mental processes. This course is challenging and utilizes a variety of activities that require and emphasize critical thinking skills. Students must be willing to meet the rigor of the required course work. Students will study the history and background of psychology, psychological research methods, the biological bases of behavior, sensation and perception, developmental psychology, cognition, personality, clinical psychology, and social psychology.

Law Education will introduce students to the complex topic of the law and how society interacts with the law. This course will challenge students to develop the knowledge and skills necessary to remedy interpersonal conflict as well as expose them to how they fit within the structure of the law. Law Education will require students to engage in independent research, develop cooperative skills by working with their peers and people who they disagree with, and navigate the unique task of reading court opinions and legal documents. This will be accomplished through investigative research projects, mock trials, and guest speaker presentations, among other interactive activities. This course will focus primarily on constitutional law while also incorporating criminal law, family law, torts/civil law, and contemporary legal issues/controversies. The semester will culminate with a month-long dive into a Moot Court where students will argue a case in front of staff members and real world attorneys. Students who are not willing to meet the rigor of the required reading, writing, and speaking skills are discouraged from enrolling.
Law Education Course Spotlight

One of the best ways to develop a deeper understanding of how and why American government operates is to compare our system to political systems around the world.  This course is intended for motivated, hard-working, highly-motivated, college-bound seniors. This course is a comprehensive study of comparing different governmental and political systems around the world, focusing on Great Britain, Mexico, Russia, Iran, China, and Nigeria. It will prepare students for the rigors of the college classroom as well as anticipated success on the College Board's Advanced Placement U. S. Comparative Government and Politics Exam. Students will develop college-level reading, writing, critical thinking, discussion, and note-taking skills. Therefore only those seniors who will remain serious and focused are encouraged to take this challenging yet rewarding course. Students signing up for this class are required to take the AP exam in May.

This semester-long course is designed to help students improve their math skills.  This course provides students with additional time to improve on the foundations needed for high school math coursework.  This must be a service listed in the student's IEP and taken during the same semester as Algebra.

This semester-long course is designed to help students improve their math skills.  This course provides students with additional time to improve on the foundations needed for high school math coursework.   This must be a service listed in the student's IEP and taken during the same semester as Geometry.
This semester-long course is designed to help students improve their reading skills by focusing on fluency, decoding skills, use of context cues, vocabulary development, and comprehension skills.

This semester-long course is designed to provide a smaller setting for students to use in place of a study hall.  This structured environment allows the student to get academic assistance with assignments, organize materials, and have more time to complete tests, quizzes, or projects.  No credit is awarded for this class.

This semester-long course is designed to provide a smaller setting for students to use in place of a study hall.  This structured environment allows the student to get academic assistance with assignments, organize materials, and have more time to complete tests, quizzes, or projects.  No credit is awarded for this class.

This semester-long course is designed to provide an even smaller setting for students to use in place of a study hall.  This structured environment allows the student to get behavioral support to develop positive relationships as well as assistance with assignments, organizing materials, and more time to complete tests, quizzes, or projects if necessary. No credit is awarded for this class.

Inclusion supports are offered in core English classes, core math classes, and select core science classes.  Placement is based on need and discussed at the case conference.  Inclusion supports include additional special education assistance offered during the core classes for assignment completion, reteaching, and accommodations on assessments.

Through a case conference, a student may be eligible for the PACE Program, Partnerships Accelerating Community Education. This is a comprehensive program developed to prepare students for entry into the world of work. This program is designed for high school students with mild disabilities, who are unable to meet the End of Course requirements outlined by the Indiana Department of Education for a high school diploma. Students in this program must earn credits in functional academics which includes Career Ready coursework: Applied English, Applied Math, Applied Science, Applied Social Studies, Lifeskills courses in the Family and Consumer Science, Career Exploration and Information, 1 Health credit; 2 Physical Education credits; and one credit in Intro to Business. Other options for our Juniors and Seniors include participation in a vocational program at Central Nine Career Center. Students will also have the opportunity to participate in community work experience. This will enrich the educational curriculum and add to their vocational experience.
Students enrolled in these classes will receive an Alternate Diploma. Students will take an Alternate Assessment and complete a Transition Portfolio upon completion of high school.

Through a case conference, a student may be eligible for the CIP Program, Comprehensive Intervention Program. This is a comprehensive program developed to meet the individual needs of students with more significant disabilities. The program is designed to meet the functional and vocational needs of each student and prepare them for independent living and the world of work. Adapted Physical Education is a component of this program; adaptive physical education will be offered during the student's freshman and sophomore years in the CIP program.
Students in this program will receive an Alternate Diploma when they exit Franklin Central High School. Students enrolled in these classes will take an alternate assessment and complete a Transition Portfolio upon completion of high school.
Students may be offered services through our SPARC Center during their years of transition 18-22 years old. The SPARC center is located on the Franklin Township campus and focuses primarily on transition skills for independent living and vocational training. Placement is determined through a case conference committee decision.

This course is an introduction to the utilization of the elements and principles of design through various media on a flat surface. These media and techniques will be used to explore and to develop observational skills, visual problem-solving, craftsmanship, and an introduction to planning and executing individual ideas and concepts.

Students in this course will study a variety of Digital Photography methods. Students will learn all about the advanced settings on their digital camera.  Students will learn tips for shooting different subject matter and creating good composition using the elements and principles of design. Students will also learn how to edit their photographs and add artistic effects using Adobe Lightroom.

Students will continue exploring a variety of media with a focus on refining skills and techniques. Assignments will continue to focus on the utilization of elements of art and principles of design. Students will further the development of their observation skills, and visual problem solving. They will unlearn bad drawing habits and experience growth in their artistic journey!

Students will learn to make multiple reproductions of a single design. They will use a variety of printmaking processes to reproduce their designs, including monoprint, cyanotype, linocut, and screenprinting.  Printmaking students will reflect upon and revise their work, relate to other disciplines and explore the historical and cultural background of each process. Students will plan most of their designs digitally, while practicing digital citizenship policies regarding copyrighted material and fair use.

This course is a reinforcement and expansion of the Advanced 2-D Art course. Students will continue exploring a variety of media and art styles as they begin to understand and refine their own artistic preferences . Assignments will continue to focus on the utilization of elements and principles of design while furthering the development of observational skills and visual problem solving. The planning and proposing of ideas and concepts for student driven work will be introduced in the second semester. Students will engage in individual and group critiques as assessment of their growth as an emerging artist.

This course is a reinforcement and expansion of the Advanced 2-D Art course. Students will continue exploring a variety of media and art styles as they begin to understand and refine their own artistic preferences . Assignments will continue to focus on the utilization of elements and principles of design while furthering the development of observational skills and visual problem solving. The planning and proposing of ideas and concepts for student driven work will be introduced in the second semester. Students will engage in individual and group critiques as assessment of their growth as an emerging artist.

Drawing/Painting II is structured so that students are working more independently.
Portfolio quality work and preparation will be a part of the course for those students wishing to apply to a university art program.
Thorough planning, proposing and execution of ideas and/or concepts for work will be a standard expectation.

Drawing/Painting II is structured so that students are working more independently.
Portfolio quality work and preparation will be a part of the course for those students wishing to apply to a university art program.
Thorough planning, proposing and execution of ideas and/or concepts for work will be a standard expectation.

Drawing/Painting III is structured so that students are working more independently.
Portfolio quality work and preparation will be a part of the course for those students wishing to apply to a university art program.
Thorough planning, proposing and execution of ideas and/or concepts for work will be a standard expectation.

Drawing/Painting III is structured so that students are working more independently.
Portfolio quality work and preparation will be a part of the course for those students wishing to apply to a university art program.
Thorough planning, proposing and execution of ideas and/or concepts for work will be a standard expectation.

The AP Studio Art classes are designed for students who are seriously interested in art. AP Studio Art is not based on a written exam; instead, students submit portfolios of their artwork for evaluation at the end of the school year. The program consists of three portfolios to choose from: Drawing, 2D Design or 3D Design.
This program provides the opportunity to earn college credit while in high school and is based on the premise that college level material can be successfully taught to secondary school students.

The AP Studio Art classes are designed for students who are seriously interested in art. AP Studio Art is not based on a written exam; instead, students submit portfolios of their artwork for evaluation at the end of the school year. The program consists of three portfolios to choose from: Drawing, 2D Design or 3D Design.
This program provides the opportunity to earn college credit while in high school and is based on the premise that college level material can be successfully taught to secondary school students.

Students in this course will create a variety of 3D objects out of clay.  Students will learn techniques like sculpting, carving, slab-building, and coil-building to develop their spatial skills.  Students will learn to enhance the surface of their projects with glaze and paint. The students will reflect upon and revise their work and use the elements and principles to create good design.

Students in this course will study ways to create art using fibrous materials. They will learn techniques such as macrame, dip-dying, needle felting, embroidery, fabric painting, sewing and more! This class focuses on using the principles of design to guide our artmaking processes.

Students in this course will be building on the skills learned in Intro 3D Art to create more complex projects. Students will learn advanced techniques for sculpting, carving, slab-building, and coil-building. Students will learn advanced techniques for enhancing the surface of their projects with glaze and paint.

Students in this course will study the creation of wearable art. This class involves the use of torches and power tools. Students will learn how to create beads, bracelets, necklaces, rings, and pendants. Students will learn a variety of simple metal working techniques like: cutting, forming, and riveting.  The students will reflect upon and revise their work and use the elements and principles to create good design.

Students in ceramics engage in sequential learning experiences that encompass art history, aesthetics, and production and lead to the creation of portfolio quality works. Students create works of art in clay utilizing the processes of hand building, molds, wheel throwing, slip and glaze techniques, and the firing processes. They reflect upon and refine their work; explore cultural and personal  connections; and incorporate literacy and presentational skills.

Students in this course will be learning advanced methods of working with 3-D materials like clay, polymer clay, stone, and wood..  Students will communicate their creative ideas and continue to develop design skills by using the elements and principles. Students are expected to begin creating high quality portfolio level work.

Students will be fully engaged in the 'Creative Process'. The 'Creative Process' is the method in which every artist chooses their own artistic adventure, investigates ideas and masters technical skills.
The works would be of portfolio quality demonstrating the artists' ability to communicate their message, their technique and their breadth of skills.

Students in this course will continue to gain mastery of 3D design using a variety of 3D materials, tools and techniques. Students will  be fully engaged in the 'Creative Process' and will make the majority of choices about what they will create in order to communicate their creative ideas and develop their artistic voice. The works would be of portfolio quality demonstrating the artists' ability to communicate their message, their technique and their breadth of skills.

The AP Studio Art course is an advanced artmaking course that demonstrates to a college admissions office that you have participated in the most challenging curriculum available. AP courses provide students with the opportunity to earn college credit, advanced placement, or both, while in high school.
AP Studio Art is an advanced-level art class where students are expected to perform at a high level in the following ways:
-Students will experiment with new ideas and new ways of approaching creative problems
-Students will conduct an in-depth exploration of a single topic, media or artistic style
-Students will showcase MASTERY through three skill categories* --
  • Inquiry & Investigation
  • Making Through Practice, Experimentation, and Revision
  • Communication and Reflection
At the end of the AP Studio Art Course, students will submit a complete portfolio to The College Board. This portfolio will be evaluated by a panel of non-partial evaluators who are college faculty or expert AP teachers, and a grade ranging from 1-5 will be assigned. The scoring range reflects the students' achievement in an equivalent college course.
What are the Portfolio Requirements?
Your portfolio exam contains two sections:
-Selected Works -- worth 40% of your grade
10 digital images consisting of two views each of five works that demonstrate synthesis of materials, processes, and ideas using [3D design] skills
-Sustained Investigation -- worth 60% of your grade
15 digital images of works of art and process documentation that demonstrate sustained investigation through practice, experimentation, and revision
Both portfolio sections will require students to submit a written explanation of their work based on the prompts given by the College Board.

Students will be fully engaged in the 'Creative Process'. The 'Creative Process' is the method in which every artist chooses their own artistic adventure, investigates ideas and masters technical skills.
The works would be of portfolio quality demonstrating the artists' ability to communicate their message, their technique and their breadth of skills.

Students in this course will continue to gain mastery of 3D design using a variety of 3D materials, tools and techniques. Students will  be fully engaged in the 'Creative Process' and will make the majority of choices about what they will create in order to communicate their creative ideas and develop their artistic voice. The works would be of portfolio quality demonstrating the artists' ability to communicate their message, their technique and their breadth of skills.

Art Appreciation is an introductory lecture course in art which explores the creative history and processes for making art that have been utilized by humankind across cultures, from the prehistoric through the modern era. Students are introduced to specific traditional and contemporary media for communication and expression.
